Here is your complete, comprehensive set of notes on Node.js architecture with the new details seamlessly integrated into the flow:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Architecture of Node.js: How It Works Internally
The entire execution flow starts with the client, who makes a request to a server. This request is first stored in the Event Queue.
Behind the scenes, Node.js uses two core building blocks to handle these requests:

• Google V8 Engine: Translates and runs your JavaScript code.

• Libuv (C++ Library): A specialized library that provides and manages the Event Loop, the Thread Pool, and all asynchronous background operations.
The Event Loop and the Event Queue
Next, the request goes to the Event Loop. The Event Loop runs on a single thread (the main thread) and constantly watches the Event Queue. It picks up incoming requests one by one and determines what kind of operation it is handling: a blocking operation (sync) or a non-blocking operation (async).
Blocking Operations (Sync) and the Thread Pool
When the Event Loop encounters a blocking operation, it offloads the task to the Thread Pool so the main thread doesn’t freeze.

• The Thread Pool: This is a collection of worker threads managed by Libuv. It is primarily used for heavy, local computational tasks like File System (fs) operations, hashing, or cryptography.

• The Worker Allocation: The request is assigned to an available thread. Once the work is done, the thread passes the result along and returns to the pool.

• The Limitation: By default, the Thread Pool only has 4 threads available. While this can be configured, it is ultimately limited by the machine’s physical CPU cores.
Non-Blocking Operations (Async) and the OS Kernel
For non-blocking operations — specifically Network Requests (like handling incoming HTTP traffic or fetching data from an external API) — Node.js does not use the limited Thread Pool at all.
Instead, it delegates the task directly to the underlying OS Kernel. Modern operating systems are highly efficient at managing multiple network connections at the kernel level. This smart separation allows Node.js to handle thousands of concurrent network connections effortlessly without exhausting its threads.
The Major Difference & The Callback Queue
The core difference between the two tracks comes down to execution flow and timing:

• Blocking operations halt the execution of any subsequent JavaScript code until the result is completely returned from the thread pool.

• Non-blocking operations allow the main thread to keep compiling and executing further code immediately while the task runs quietly in the background.
When an asynchronous task (whether handled by a worker thread or the OS Kernel) finishes its job, it doesn’t immediately interrupt what the main thread is doing. Instead, its corresponding Callback Function is placed into the Callback Queue.
The Event Loop continuously monitors the main execution thread. The absolute moment the main thread becomes completely empty and finishes executing the initial script, the Event Loop pulls the callback function from the Callback Queue and executes it, delivering the final answer to the application.
